CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): The paper is very well organized with a logical flow from introduction through method, experiments, and conclusion, and the main contributions (TagLM and bidirectional LM embeddings improving NER/Chunking) are stated clearly early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (including F1 and perplexity results) are informative for assessing claims. The writing quality is reported as good with no ambiguity or grammatical issues in the provided summary. From a reproducibility-minded reader’s perspective, presentation is a pleasure to read and enables follow-up work. | B(4): The paper is well organized (introduction → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (TagLM approach and empirical gains on CoNLL NER and Chunking). Notation and methodological description appear consistent (separate forward/backward LMs, concatenated embeddings, CRF output) and hyperparameters are reported; figures/tables reporting F1 means/SD and LM perplexity are informative. Writing quality is good and related work coverage thorough, though the summary notes no stated limitations which could have been made more explicit for clarity.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): This paper addresses a central problem—how to leverage large unlabeled corpora to improve sequence tagging—and provides clear, broadly applicable evidence that pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings substantially boost performance (new state-of-the-art on CoNLL-2003 NER and CoNLL-2000 chunking, with mean F1 gains ~1–1.4 and larger relative gains in low-resource settings). From my reproducibility-focused perspective, the approach (separate forward/backward LMs trained on the 1B Word benchmark, concatenated token embeddings injected into a CRF-based tagger) is simple, general, and likely to change practitioners’ default architectures and spawn many follow-ups, so its impact should be transformative for the field. | B(4): This paper tackles an important practical problem—improving sequence tagging—by demonstrating that pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings (trained on the 1B Word Benchmark) yield consistent, substantial F1 gains and new state-of-the-art results on CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 Chunking without extra labels or gazetteers. The approach is likely to change practice because it provides a simple, general mechanism to inject contextualized, pretrained representations into supervised taggers and shows especially large benefits in low-resource settings. It also opens clear follow-ons (different pretraining data, model sizes, and integration strategies) and is likely to be widely adopted, though its reliance on large-scale LM pretraining and additional inference cost tempers an argument for transformative impact across all settings.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a central problem—how to leverage large unlabeled corpora to improve sequence tagging—and provides clear, broadly applicable evidence that pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings substantially boost performance (new state-of-the-art on CoNLL-2003 NER and CoNLL-2000 chunking, with mean F1 gains ~1–1.4 and larger relative gains in low-resource settings). From my reproducibility-focused perspective, the approach (separate forward/backward LMs trained on the 1B Word benchmark, concatenated token embeddings injected into a CRF-based tagger) is simple, general, and likely to change practitioners’ default architectures and spawn many follow-ups, so its impact should be transformative for the field.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—improving sequence tagging—by demonstrating that pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings (trained on the 1B Word Benchmark) yield consistent, substantial F1 gains and new state-of-the-art results on CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 Chunking without extra labels or gazetteers. The approach is likely to change practice because it provides a simple, general mechanism to inject contextualized, pretrained representations into supervised taggers and shows especially large benefits in low-resource settings. It also opens clear follow-ons (different pretraining data, model sizes, and integration strategies) and is likely to be widely adopted, though its reliance on large-scale LM pretraining and additional inference cost tempers an argument for transformative impact across all settings.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper proposes a pragmatic and clearly articulated idea—augmenting sequence taggers with concatenated, independently pretrained forward and backward LM embeddings—that yields strong empirical gains and new SOTA on CoNLL datasets. While the specific use of large pretrained bidirectional LMs as token-level features and the independence of the forward/backward models appear to be novel operational choices relative to prior transfer/joint-learning work, the overall approach is an incremental composition of existing elements (language modeling, pretrained embeddings, hierarchical taggers with CRF). From an expert reproducibility perspective the contribution is useful and practically informative, but not a highly radical methodological departure from prior semi-supervised transfer techniques. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly articulated and practically novel approach—TagLM—by pretraining large forward and backward neural LMs on the 1B Word Benchmark, extracting top-layer token embeddings, concatenating them as bidirectional LM features, and feeding them into a CRF-based sequence tagger. This is meaningfully different from prior transfer or joint-learning methods (the authors demonstrate larger gains than earlier transfer approaches and achieve new SOTA on CoNLL NER and chunking without extra labeled data), and the use of independently trained forward/backward LMs is a distinct methodological choice. While the idea of leveraging pretrained LMs for supervision is an extension of transfer learning trends, the specific formulation and empirical gains make this more than an incremental combination of existing techniques and would be informative to experts.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a pragmatic and clearly articulated idea—augmenting sequence taggers with concatenated, independently pretrained forward and backward LM embeddings—that yields strong empirical gains and new SOTA on CoNLL datasets. From an expert reproducibility perspective the contribution is useful and practically informative, but not a highly radical methodological departure from prior semi-supervised transfer techniques.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference: it proposes a broadly relevant semi-supervised method (TagLM) that leverages large-scale pretrained bidirectional language models and demonstrates state-of-the-art gains on standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2003 NER, CoNLL 2000 Chunking). The experimental setup uses accepted datasets, reports mean F1 with standard deviation, compares to strong baselines, and provides hyperparameter details, matching the venue’s methodological and evaluation expectations. From a reproducibility perspective I note code release is not mentioned, but the paper otherwise positions itself correctly for the NLP/ML community and addresses the right audience. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it proposes a clearly scoped, practically important method (TagLM) that leverages large-scale bidirectional LM pretraining to improve sequence tagging, and demonstrates new state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 Chunking). The methodology (pretraining on the 1B Word Benchmark, integrating top-layer LM embeddings into a hierarchical RNN+CRF tagger) and evaluation (mean F1 with baselines including strong recent models) meet the community’s standards. The topic targets the right audience—researchers working on transfer, representation learning, and sequence tagging—and is well positioned within related work on pretrained LMs and semi-supervised learning.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a strong set of recent baselines (Chiu & Nichols 2016; Hashimoto et al. 2016; Yang et al. 2017; Ma & Hovy 2016; Lample et al. 2016) and a sensible internal baseline (bidirectional RNN+CRF), and evaluates on standard datasets (CoNLL 2003 NER, CoNLL 2000 Chunking) using F1 with reported means and standard deviations. Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the authors explicitly contrast bidirectional LM embeddings with previous transfer/joint-learning methods and report LM perplexity, indicating thoughtful analysis. Minor gaps remain for full reproducibility and ablation depth (e.g., more extensive sensitivity analyses, multiple random seeds across settings, and explicit admission where prior methods match or exceed results), preventing a top score. | B(5): The paper compares to a comprehensive set of strong and recent baselines (Chiu & Nichols 2016; Hashimoto et al. 2016; Yang et al. 2017; Ma & Hovy 2016; Lample et al. 2016) on the same standard datasets (CoNLL 2003 NER, CoNLL 2000 Chunking) using the same F1 evaluation metric, reporting mean F1 and standard deviations. It also situates gains relative to prior transfer and joint-learning methods and reports LM perplexity for the pretrained model, making the comparisons both fair and informative. The related-work coverage is described as thorough and competing approaches are cited and discussed, supporting the claim of improved state-of-the-art performance. Overall the experimental comparisons are comprehensive and appropriately controlled.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a strong set of recent baselines (Chiu & Nichols 2016; Hashimoto et al. 2016; Yang et al. 2017; Ma & Hovy 2016; Lample et al. 2016) and a sensible internal baseline (bidirectional RNN+CRF), and evaluates on standard datasets (CoNLL 2003 NER, CoNLL 2000 Chunking) using F1 with reported means and standard deviations. Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and the authors explicitly contrast bidirectional LM embeddings with previous transfer/joint-learning methods and report LM perplexity, indicating thoughtful analysis. Minor gaps remain for full reproducibility and ablation depth (e.g., more extensive sensitivity analyses, multiple random seeds across settings, and explicit admission where prior methods match or exceed results), preventing a top score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to a comprehensive set of strong and recent baselines (Chiu & Nichols 2016; Hashimoto et al. 2016; Yang et al. 2017; Ma & Hovy 2016; Lample et al. 2016) on the same standard datasets (CoNLL 2003 NER, CoNLL 2000 Chunking) using the same F1 evaluation metric, reporting mean F1 and standard deviations. It also situates gains relative to prior transfer and joint-learning methods and reports LM perplexity for the pretrained model, making the comparisons both fair and informative. SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main empirical claims are clearly stated and largely supported: they pretrain forward/backward LMs on the 1B Word Benchmark, incorporate top-layer LM embeddings into a hierarchical tagger with CRF, and report improved mean F1 (CoNLL-2003 NER 91.93, CoNLL-2000 Chunking 96.37) with standard deviations and LM perplexity for the pretrained LMs. Experimental methodology is generally sound—strong baselines are included and hyperparameters are reported—but critical reproducibility elements are missing (code release not mentioned) and some desiderata for a reproducible-methodologist remain unaddressed (comprehensive ablations isolating each component, more details on random seeds and multiple runs/statistical significance beyond std reporting, and explicit comparisons for choices like layer selection or CRF vs softmax). Overall the results justify the conclusions, but minor gaps in ablation and reproducibility prevent a top score. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments on CoNLL-2003 and CoNLL-2000 showing consistent F1 improvements from adding pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings (with reported means and stddevs) and comparison to appropriate strong baselines (including a strong hierarchical RNN+CRF baseline and prior state-of-the-art systems). There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations, so completeness is not an issue, and the methodology—pretraining on the 1B Word Benchmark and augmenting a supervised tagger—is sound. Minor concerns remain: formal significance testing is not reported and some design choices (e.g., separate vs. joint bidirectional LMs) could be explored more deeply, but these do not undermine the primary empirical claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main empirical claims are clearly stated and largely supported: they pretrain forward/backward LMs on the 1B Word Benchmark, incorporate top-layer LM embeddings into a hierarchical tagger with CRF, and report improved mean F1 (CoNLL-2003 NER 91.93, CoNLL-2000 Chunking 96.37) with standard deviations and LM perplexity for the pretrained LMs. Experimental methodology is generally sound—strong baselines are included and hyperparameters are reported—but critical reproducibility elements are missing (code release not mentioned) and some desiderata for a reproducible-methodologist remain unaddressed (comprehensive ablations isolating each component, more details on random seeds and multiple runs/statistical significance beyond std reporting, and explicit comparisons for choices like layer selection or CRF vs softmax). Overall the results justify the conclusions, but minor gaps in ablation and reproducibility prevent a top score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments on CoNLL-2003 and CoNLL-2000 showing consistent F1 improvements from adding pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings (with reported means and stddevs) and comparison to appropriate strong baselines (including a strong hierarchical RNN+CRF baseline and prior state-of-the-art systems). There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations, so completeness is not an issue, and the methodology—pretraining on the 1B Word Benchmark and augmenting a supervised tagger—is sound. Minor concerns remain: formal significance testing is not reported and some design choices (e.g., separate vs. joint bidirectional LMs) could be explored more deeply, but these do not undermine the primary empirical claims.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents strong empirical evidence (SOTA on CoNLL2003 and CoNLL2000, mean F1 with standard deviations, LM perplexity) and includes useful experiments such as low-resource sampling and comparison to prior transfer/joint-learning approaches. Hyperparameters are reported and the LM was pretrained on a large corpus, but the summary does not indicate release of code or complete reproducibility artifacts (random seeds, exact preprocessing), and some important ablations are missing or unclear (e.g., explicit forward vs. backward contribution analyses, layer selection, CRF vs. softmax). Related work coverage is reported as thorough, but the paper fails to discuss limitations or failure cases. Overall the experimental design is solid and convincing, with minor gaps that prevent a fully exhaustive reproducibility and analysis assessment. | B(4): The paper provides solid empirical support for its main claims: experiments on CoNLL-2003 NER and CoNLL-2000 chunking show consistent, significant gains (state-of-the-art F1s reported), include low-resource (1% sampling) analyses, and compare forward, backward, and concatenated bidirectional LM embeddings against strong baselines (including prior transfer/joint-learning methods). Related work appears to be covered thoroughly and hyperparameters / LM perplexity are reported, aiding reproducibility, though code and some pretraining details (beyond using the 1B Word Benchmark and CNN-BIG-LSTM) are not mentioned. The manuscript omits discussion of limitations or failure cases and is somewhat narrow in scope (two sequence-tagging tasks and one large unlabeled corpus), so while the experiments are convincing they stop short of exhaustive analysis across more tasks and datasets.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents strong empirical evidence (SOTA on CoNLL2003 and CoNLL2000, mean F1 with standard deviations, LM perplexity) and includes useful experiments such as low-resource sampling and comparison to prior transfer/joint-learning approaches. Hyperparameters are reported and the LM was pretrained on a large corpus, but the summary does not indicate release of code or complete reproducibility artifacts (random seeds, exact preprocessing), and some important ablations are missing or unclear (e.g., explicit forward vs. backward contribution analyses, layer selection, CRF vs. softmax). Related work coverage is reported as thorough, but the paper fails to discuss limitations or failure cases. Overall the experimental design is solid and convincing, with minor gaps that prevent a fully exhaustive reproducibility and analysis assessment.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides solid empirical support for its main claims: experiments on CoNLL-2003 NER and CoNLL-2000 chunking show consistent, significant gains (state-of-the-art F1s reported), include low-resource (1% sampling) analyses, and compare forward, backward, and concatenated bidirectional LM embeddings against strong baselines (including prior transfer/joint-learning methods). Related work appears to be covered thoroughly and hyperparameters / LM perplexity are reported, aiding reproducibility, though code and some pretraining details (beyond using the 1B Word Benchmark and CNN-BIG-LSTM) are not mentioned. The manuscript omits discussion of limitations or failure cases and is somewhat narrow in scope (two sequence-tagging tasks and one large unlabeled corpus), so while the experiments are convincing they stop short of exhaustive analysis across more tasks and datasets.

main_claims: Introducing TagLM: augmenting supervised sequence taggers with pretrained bidirectional language model embeddings improves performance.
TagLM achieves new state-of-the-art results on CoNLL 2003 NER (91.93 mean F1) and CoNLL 2000 Chunking (96.37 mean F1) without using additional labeled data or gazetteers.

LM embeddings provide substantial absolute F1 gains (average improvements of 1.06 for NER and 1.37 for Chunking) and larger relative gains in low-resource settings (e.g., sampling 1% of training data).

Bidirectional LM embeddings (concatenation of independently trained forward and backward LMs) are more beneficial than previous transfer or joint-learning methods and often outperform gains from adding external resources.
_summary.methodology: Pretrain separate forward and backward neural language models on large unlabeled corpora (1B Word Benchmark), extract top-layer LM embeddings for each token, concatenate forward and backward embeddings to form bidirectional LM embeddings, and input these as additional features into a supervised hierarchical neural sequence tagger with a CRF output layer.
baselines: baseline hierarchical neural sequence tagging model (bidirectional RNN + CRF)
metrics: Language model perplexity (reported for CNN-BIG-LSTM: 30.0 test perplexity)
_summary.topic_area: NLP - semi-supervised sequence tagging using pretrained bidirectional language models

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: ML engineer and researcher with extensive experience building and evaluating production-scale NLP systems and running large empirical studies; frequent reviewer on reproducibility and experimental methodology for top ML/NLP conferences.
traits: values clear, exact experimental protocols (training data splits, hyperparameters, random seeds) and code/data release to enable replication
expects comprehensive baselines and ablations that isolate the contribution of each component (e.g., forward vs backward LMs, layer selection, embedding dimensionality, CRF vs softmax)
looks for statistical rigor: multiple runs with variance reporting and significance testing when claiming state-of-the-art
checks for fair comparisons to prior work (same data preprocessing, OOV handling, and external resources) and sensitivity analyses (low-resource settings, domain shifts)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Carefully inspects experimental design and empirical claims, demanding reproducibility, rigorous ablations, and clear evidence that reported improvements are robust and attributable to the proposed method.

reviewer_b.name: Representation & Impact Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher focused on learned representations, transfer learning, and the broader impacts of model design on downstream tasks; advisor of multiple papers that introduced widely used pretrained models and transfer methods.
traits: evaluates novelty and contribution relative to the literature on transfer, feature-based vs fine-tuning approaches, and prior LM-based methods
assesses the practical significance and generality of results (e.g., does this meaningfully change practice for NER/chunking and beyond?)
notices scalability, cost, and deployment considerations (pretraining corpus size, compute, inference overhead of adding LM embeddings)
values clarity about limitations, potential negative consequences, and guidance for when to prefer this approach over alternatives
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Focuses on placing the work in context, judging whether the idea advances the field and offering a balanced view of its practical impact, novelty, and limitations.
